speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] with Mrs. Hait, Zelda Hait. The interview takes place on February 25, 1997 by Nina [INAUDIBLE], in [INAUDIBLE], Israel, in the English language. We just finished, in the last cassette, speaking about [INAUDIBLE]. Who else, from the people that you knew in Riga, who were connected, somehow, to [INAUDIBLE] and to your period of hiding, whom could you mention? What do you know about them? What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. The people who exactly knew that I was hiding were Laimons Lidums and Edgars Kraujinsh who shared our apartment. One of them is, allegedly, alive in Toronto. I'm not in touch with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Laimons Lidums. And Edgars Kraujinsh died of a kidney ailment, as a result of a abuse of alcohol-- sev-- about five or six years ago, I believe. Who else? Now, there is a man whom I was trying to find that was [INAUDIBLE]. He lived in-- in the United States, had been living there for very many years. He was, allegedly, extradited from the country. He settled down in Canada, and now I read in the newspapers that Canada has also extrad-- extradited him. He's also a schoolmate of [INAUDIBLE]. Together, they graduated from military school. He was involved in Jew killing in Riga-- and not only in Riga, in other places as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I went on a pre-trial investigation, to Philadelphia, in the year 1980. It was a long investigation, which lasted over four ye-- for four hours. I was cross-examined by his lawyer, a Latvian by the name of Berzinsh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-R-Z-I-N-S-H. He was rather anti-Semitic. I felt his antipathy towards me. But Professor [INAUDIBLE]-- from Professor [INAUDIBLE], in a letter, I heard that he's a man who has an absolutely different view of the Holocaust, and that he has got his own version of the Holocaust in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: To [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. There is-- there are several versions about it. One is that he died peacefully. The other one is that he comm-- has committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was 1981.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you give any testimonies in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. Inform-- on several occasions and informative trips to Richard Sullivan and Robin, and about [INAUDIBLE], Karklinsh, Sprogis, Laipenieks, and many others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hang on. I want you to spell all the names, because--

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because otherwise it will be confusion.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Sprogis is S-P-R-O-G-I-S, Sprogis. Karklinsh is K-A-R-K-L-I-N-S-H. Laipenieks is a well-known [INAUDIBLE] in Latvian-- L-A-I-P-E-N-I-E-K-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Department of Justice do any preparation for a trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was an-- a repeated investigation, and they were about to bring these people to trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you what happened. I've heard different versions. But since I don't know the whole truth, I am not entitled to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: With whom were you in contact, and what kind of witness did you give to the Department of Justice about these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them everything I knew about the role they played, during the time of the German occupation. It was killing and torturing people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the information from the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got the information, in the first place, by [INAUDIBLE], himself, whose friends have been involved, and by Lidums and Kraujinsh, in Nazi-occupied Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we-- we forgot to mention the name of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, but he was a man who was killed by Israeli agents. And there was no-- on behalf of me, there was no participation in that case.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about the meeting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. On one of the evenings when there were-- [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], and the rest, were celebrating again, something in the living room. About midnight, someone else appeared. There were many, many people. And the man who appeared-- I didn't see him, I only heard him. And I heard that [INAUDIBLE] had arrived in a leather coat, and he was holding a pistol in his hand. This was [INAUDIBLE] called me and told me. And he said, with this pistol, I have killed 300 people, Jews, today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell this, also, to the federal Department of Justice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Yes, I did. But unfortunately, it was after [INAUDIBLE] death.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were the investigations, or the informative investigations, held in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely different than the German. In the first place, there are different laws and rules. I was questioned, and I had to tell all the facts-- where the hotel room where I was staying. There was also a cross-examination, and so that I was prepared to go to trial, and to know exactly what the facts-- what facts I may expected to be, to tell. And everything was on a very friendly basis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So trial for whom, did they prepare?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first place, for [INAUDIBLE]. And then they were interested in Lidums in [INAUDIBLE], in Sprogis, in Laipenieks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who is still alive, from all these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you, because I have not asked anyone. I'm not in touch with the police anymore. Those people I was in touch with have left. They are retired, and the police station somebody [INAUDIBLE] now, and the people are different.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after-- while you were involved in [INAUDIBLE] trial, did you have any effects from the Latvian people living in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. [INAUDIBLE] called me, whenever I was staying in my daughter's house in Buffalo. And he threatened me, in the first place, that his niece works as Kissinger's secretary, which is a lie-- and then, that he will never permit me to tread, with my dirty boots, on Latvian soil, and tell the Latvian people. And if I go on doing it, he will kill me-- except in Latvian it sounds different-- kill me. I-- I informed the police about it, and the German police, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing happened. He's afraid. He's scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him, over the same time when you were there, in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, of course, I did see him. I had got a letter from him. I didn't know where he was. He was looking for me. He was looking, and as far as I understood, he was looking for an alibi, that he was a nice guy during the German times, because he was scared. And he thought that I would be a very good witness in his favor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and finally, my daughter lived not so far away. She lived in Buffalo, and he lived in Toronto. And one day they appeared, and they invited us to their house. So I did go to their house, because I wanted to know everything that is of great interest to me. So I was, several times, in their house. With whom did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my husband, [INAUDIBLE], and my son-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was it? Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very friendly, very forthcoming-- hospitality, top hospitality. And then they asked me to come with them to their neighbor, who is a Jew, and who owns a shop. So I didn't know for what purpose. I went in, and he said, here is a young-- the lady whose life I saved when she was young. Do you understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you approve?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he speak to you about the time of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, a lot. He said I was young and stupid. This is why I did it. And then he said, on another occasion, that all Jews are [INAUDIBLE], which is Freemasons. And Stalin was a Jew, and Churchill was a Jew, and the Jews are the-- are of the misery of the world, et cetera, et cetera. It depended on the amount of vodka he had drunk. Lost control.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel, to be in his house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't feel anything. I only knew I had a purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: The purpose was to hear where his friends live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he give you any information?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not-- he didn't do it on his own free will. He just, he was just chatting, chatting without an end, and telling me that he's in touch with this friend. I say, oh, yes. And he's a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are the friends you called a report about, to the Department of Justice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened afterwards, after you have been to his house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We just broke off. I broke off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this threat came afterward?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The threat came-- after the threat, I broke off my relationship with him. I said, these are not the German times, when you can threaten me or kill me. You are a refug-- a refugee in Canada, and watch out. Watch out. And he-- he got the message. And that was the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel any security from the American side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. They really kept an eye on me all the time, took me to the airport and took me, even, to the plane. And they met me at the plane, and holding hands-- we were rushing to the other plane, to Philadelphia. And in Philadelphia, itself, also they didn't give me a chance to be by my-- on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the investigation last?

speaker: interviewee
text: In America? For hours, for hours-- the [INAUDIBLE] organization, over four hours. But at the hotel, getting ready for it, two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your feelings when you knew that [INAUDIBLE] couldn't come to trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No feelings at all. I thought that the son of a bitch has-- has done so many ugly things that I didn't want to think about him. I think that he has spent the last 10 years, at least, in fear. And this is what I am interested in. I don't want him to die peacefully. I want him to die in terror, in danger. That's-- that's the only thing I can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you fill any reports in, for the American Department of Justice?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean by reports?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or only verb-- was it verbal, or oral reports, or written?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was giving evidence? Everything was written down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to these papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: These papers got lost. It's not only these papers. There were tapes of the Philadelphia trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of [INAUDIBLE]. And all the trial of Germany was into German safe. A copy of the trial was in a safe in the Justice Department. And one day, Dr. [INAUDIBLE] Dick Sullivan called me to [INAUDIBLE] house, telling me that your-- all your papers have disappeared. Your testimony has disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know why?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very, very upset. And he says, he has no idea how this would happen in the Justice Department, in a safe. So I had my thoughts, and I-- I didn't express my thoughts. And I believe 100% that I was right, because here I have a letter, written by Professor [INAUDIBLE], where he says that he has been following me in archives for so many years, et cetera, et cetera, knowing everything, absolutely, about my life and about my existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this professor.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know him. I have never seen him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is a historian. Only then, Sullivan told me that historians have access to the safe. Historians have access. He was interested to build a free-- free Latvia. Now they have their independence. And he wanted me to help him make propaganda for Latvia, that only a dozen and a half of Latvians were involved in war crimes, the rest were wonderful people and lived hand-in-hand with the Jewish people. Whatever he wrote was a big lie. He even quoted [INAUDIBLE] in a very positive sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: Saying what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saying what? That the Germans have misled the Latvian people, promising them a lot, but that after the war they wouldn't get anything. And they would have been slaves, etc. Meaningless, meaningless-- but he shouldn't have vot-- quoted [INAUDIBLE] in a positive sense, and that was appalling, absolutely. People ask me why I don't answer him. I should have answered him. And I wrote down an answer, which I didn't send, that there are people with whom I don't want to be a pen friend, and that is Professor [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he write you this letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many years ago. I think it was 1987 or '88-- many years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all this letter consists of denial of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: But how do you feel about them generally?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same question you could ask me, what, how do you feel about anti-Semites. They are either neo-nazis, who are still sharing the views of those who have killed, and would have seen another [INAUDIBLE]. And it's either, or they are absolutely limited, primitive people who have no idea what-- what is going-- what has gone on. And I don't think a lot about them, to tell you the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything left to testify against?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're approaching the end of the interview. You've been involved, and you had [INAUDIBLE], and I'm sure you have a message. What is your message?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my message, I know exactly this. It is what I said to my granddaughter now, when she was leaving to see the death camps of Poland. It will be terrible for you to see it, and this is what really has happened to our nation. And now, you-- all the young generation-- should know how much we should appreciate what we have-- this small land and our army. That is the message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with pleasure. On my right is sitting my daughter, Naomi, who is 45 now. Behind me is-- are my two granddaughters. On the left is Osnot, who is 23. And on the right is Efrat, who is 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lovely. I would like to ask Naomi or the girls, or all of them, a question-- mainly Naomi-- a trivial one. How is your reaction today, or how was your reaction and your education, living in a house of a history like m-- mother-- like your mother-- Holocaust survivor, and whatever she went through in the Soviet Union?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING HEBREW]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING HEBREW]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING HEBREW]

text: [SPEAKING HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING HEBREW] This is a historic building in the old city of Riga called [INAUDIBLE], where-- where the Latvians were asked, on the first day of the German occupation, to-- to turn up with their weapons, in order to kill the Jews and communists. That's a picture of me, shortly after I had escaped from the ghetto in 1941. That's my old nanny, the one who has brought me up, and later helped me bring up my baby when I was arrested, [INAUDIBLE]. That is a picture of [INAUDIBLE], the man who has, risking his own life, saved mine, during the time of the German occupation. That's the infamous Jew killer, Victors [INAUDIBLE], who was arrested in Germany after the war, and got a life sentence-- died in prison after 10 years of imprisonment. Authorities of the Central Prison of Riga, to [INAUDIBLE], means to me, that after having been in prison for over a year there, I am being sent to my place of residence at a psychiatric hospital of the city of Riga. This is a comb, which I was obliged to use in Novosibirsk, in a terrible prison, where nothing else was available. [SPEAKING HEBREW] Canada. [SPEAKING HEBREW]. [SPEAKING HEBREW]. [SPEAKING HEBREW]. Chicago, [SPEAKING HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? --his ?] beloved mommy. Don't forget that you come from a long-suffering nation. What does a nation have to be afraid of that has 40 years wandered through the desert, and that has survived Egypt and Babylon? Don't forget that you are a Jew, and that your suffering is just a drop in the sea of general suffering. [INAUDIBLE].
